{"author":{"id":"MDQ6VXNlcjUwNzQ1","is_bot":false,"login":"cmungall","name":"Chris Mungall"},"body":"CL currently has hierarchies for neuroblast (vertebrate) and NPC:

* [] CL:0000055 ! non-terminally differentiated cell "A precursor cell with a limited number of potential fates."
    * [i] **CL:0000031 ! neuroblast (sensu Vertebrata) "A cell that will develop into a neuron often after a migration phase."**
        * [i] CL:0002362 ! cerebellar granule cell precursor "A cell located in the outermost proliferative zone of the external germinal layer that can differentiate into astroglial cells and granule cells. This cell type is glial fibrillary acidic protein-positive and HNK1-positive."
        * [i] CL:1000042 ! forebrain neuroblast
        * [i] CL:0002676 ! neural crest derived neuroblast "A neuroblast derived from a neural crest cell."
    * [i] **CL:0011020 ! neural progenitor cell "An undifferentiated cell that originates from a neural stem cell and has the capacity to generate multiple types of lineage-restricted progenitors but not to self-renew."**
        * [i] CL:0000469 ! ganglion mother cell "A neural progenitor cell that is the daughter of a neuroblast (sensu arthopoda).  The progeny of ganglion mother cells develop into neurons, glia and (occasionally) epithelial cells."

I'm not sure I could use these definitions to determine if something is a NPC or a NB or both. Suspiciously, the NPC branch only has a single child, which is arthropod-specific.

Our definition of neuroblast differs quite a bit from the more precise wikipedia def from which it was sourced:

_In vertebrates, a neuroblast or primitive nerve cell[[1]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Neuroblast#cite_note-Sadler-1) is a [postmitotic cell](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/G0_phase) that does not divide further,[[2]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Neuroblast#cite_note-Williams-2) and which will develop into a [neuron](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Neuron) after a [migration](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cell_migration) phase. ... Vertebrate neuroblasts [differentiate](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cellular_differentiation) from [radial glial cells](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radial_glial_cell) and are committed to becoming neurons_

We also lack the relationship to RGCs.

Note that despite the definition of NPC stating it derives from a neural stem cell, there is no such relationship in CL

Is the concept of neuroblast even useful in vertebrates any more? This recent review of NPC terminology (used in the definition of NPC) barely mentions the term despite talking about NPC cells migrating to the SVZ:
https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC6291443/

I see cellxgene subsets on a lot of these terms - does this mean they are being used to annotate single cell data? are there not more precise concepts that would be more informative?

Some general recommendations

- rather than using a pick and mix of sources for definitions and terminology, we try and focus on recent broad reviews such as PMC6291443
- we have fewer grouping concepts, and less overlapping ways to group things
- all grouping concepts should have a strategy for ensuring consistent and Reasonably Complete population, such as logical definitions, and precise grouping criteria (e.g. "often" is useless in the non-gloss part of the definition)

Specific actions

~- [ ] consider placing NB (V) under NPC~
- [ ] Clarify definition of NPC to make clear it refers to cells that divide to form...
- [ ] add GMC develops_from NB (invertebrate)
- [ ] consider: add NB develops_from RGC
- [ ] add text definition for forebrain NB
- [ ] make the definition of NB (V) the same as the wikipedia page it sources (or find a review article)","comments":[{"id":"IC_kwDOAjLh-853LAHr","author":{"login":"dosumis"},"authorAssociation":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"> Suspiciously, the NPC branch only has a single child, which is arthropod-specific.

It is worth bearing in mind that CL has branches that are hooks for a wider classification of terms in SS ontologies.  The CL graph alone is misleading here.  There are other cell types that fit this description in invertebrates, e.g. SMCs (Sensory Mother Cells).  Not sure whether the mappings are in place for this, but they should be.","createdAt":"2024-03-15T10:33:03Z","includesCreatedEdit":false,"isMinimized":false,"minimizedReason":"","reactionGroups":[],"url":"https://github.com/obophenotype/cell-ontology/issues/2312#issuecomment-1999372779","viewerDidAuthor":false},{"id":"IC_kwDOAjLh-853LBib","author":{"login":"dosumis"},"authorAssociation":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"I think an important distinction that is being missed here is that vertebrate NBs are neurons prior to differentiation.  The mother cells classified under 'neural progenitor cell' divide asymmetrically through 1 or 2 rounds to produce cells that then differentiate into neurons, glial, epithelial cells.  Lineage is determinate.

I have edited this proposed tasks to fit.","createdAt":"2024-03-15T10:36:49Z","includesCreatedEdit":true,"isMinimized":false,"minimizedReason":"","reactionGroups":[{"content":"THUMBS_UP","users":{"totalCount":1}}],"url":"https://github.com/obophenotype/cell-ontology/issues/2312#issuecomment-1999378587","viewerDidAuthor":false},{"id":"IC_kwDOAjLh-86Lx5Cb","author":{"login":"github-actions"},"authorAssociation":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"This issue has not seen any activity in the past 6 months; it will be closed automatically in one year from now if no action is taken.","createdAt":"2024-09-12T02:05:57Z","includesCreatedEdit":false,"isMinimized":false,"minimizedReason":"","reactionGroups":[],"url":"https://github.com/obophenotype/cell-ontology/issues/2312#issuecomment-2345111707","viewerDidAuthor":false},{"id":"IC_kwDOAjLh-86crD_F","author":{"login":"raymond91125"},"authorAssociation":"NONE","body":"Are there actions planned for this issue? {"body":"**CL term**
non-terminally differentiated cell (CL:0000055) and terminally differentiated osteoblast (CL:0001039)

**Suggested revision of class hierarchy**
Currently, "non-terminally differentiated cell" (CL:0000055) is an ancestor of "terminally differentiated osteoblast" (CL:0001039), which seems incorrect to me.
I think the issue is osteoblast (CL:0000062) isa Subclass of "non-terminally differentiated cell", even though there is a "non-terminally differentiated osteoblast" child of osteoblast. I'm not sure whether these 2 children of "osteoblast" should be deleted or the connection between "osteoblast" and "non-terminally differentiated cell" should be deleted.","comments":[{"id":"IC_kwDOAjLh-86dN9sj","author":{"login":"dosumis"},"authorAssociation":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"Seems to be based on the assumption that 'blast' indicated non-terminally differentiated.

Some possible fixes

## Option 1

Merge

"terminally differentiated odontoblast" --> odontocyte
"terminally differentiated osteoblast" --> osteocyte

Merge "terminally differentiated odontoblast" --> odontoblast

etc.

However, from reading around it seems that existence of odontocytes is controversial & maybe not all osteoblasts end up as osteocytes.

## Option 2: 

Obsolete the general grouping term (I'm not sure how useful it is and it is incompletely populated right now anyway)

non-terminally differentiated cell (CL:0000055) and terminally differentiated osteoblast (CL:0001039)

**Suggested revision of class hierarchy**
Currently, "non-terminally differentiated cell" (CL:0000055) is an ancestor of "terminally differentiated osteoblast" (CL:0001039), which seems incorrect to me.
I think the issue is osteoblast (CL:0000062) isa Subclass of "non-terminally differentiated cell", even though there is a "non-terminally differentiated osteoblast" child of osteoblast. I'm not sure whether these 2 children of "osteoblast" should be deleted or the connection between "osteoblast" and "non-terminally differentiated cell" should be deleted.","comments":[{"id":"IC_kwDOAjLh-86dN9sj","author":{"login":"dosumis"},"authorAssociation":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"Seems to be based on the assumption that 'blast' indicated non-terminally differentiated.

Some possible fixes

## Option 1

Merge

"terminally differentiated odontoblast" --> odontocyte
"terminally differentiated osteoblast" --> osteocyte

Merge "terminally differentiated odontoblast" --> odontoblast

etc.

However, from reading around it seems that existence of odontocytes is controversial & maybe not all osteoblasts end up as osteocytes.

## Option 2: 

Obsolete the general grouping term (I'm not sure how useful it is and it is incompletely populated right now anyway)

Especially capture: (1) how vertebrate NPC and neuroblast are distinguished, (2) whether vertebrate neuroblast is post-mitotic or proliferative, (3) the lineage relationships (RGC -> NPC, NPC -> neuroblast?). content.content:
# Neural Progenitor Cell Terminology: Key Definitions

## Core Definitions

**Neural Progenitor Cells (NPCs):** Described as "progenitor cells of the CNS that give rise to many, if not all, of the glial and neuronal cell types that populate the CNS."

**Radial Glial Cells (RG):** The primary NPCs in many developing brain regions, characterized by radial morphology and capacity for both symmetric and asymmetric divisions.

**Intermediate Progenitor (IP) Cells:** Secondary progenitors located in the subventricular zone that express Tbr2 and typically display multipolar morphology, distinguishing them from bipolar RG cells.

## Lineage Relationships

The document describes a clear two-step amplification pathway: "RG cells divide in the VZ to produce IP cells; and (2) IP cells divide in the SVZ to produce pairs of daughter neurons."

This represents the lineage as: **Radial Glia → Intermediate Progenitors → Neurons**

## Neuroblast Definition and Status

The document does not use the term "neuroblast" prominently in modern terminology. Instead, it describes "newborn neuronal cells that express doublecortin" as post-mitotic or post-division products rather than a distinct proliferative class.

The focus on "Type 2 cells" (IP cells) emphasizes that intermediate progenitors—not neuroblasts—represent the transitional proliferative stage between stem cells and differentiated neurons.

## Vertebrate NPC Distinction

The document notes that "primary NPCs across a number of CNS structures share fundamental characteristics" and mentions findings in non-mammalian vertebrates, suggesting conservation of NPC identity across vertebrate groups.

input.prompt: What is the current Wikipedia definition of neuroblast (vertebrate)? Quote it directly and verbatim. Is a neuroblast post-mitotic? Does it divide further? What does it develop from? Does it migrate? Quote key passages with citations.

content.content:
# Neuroblast Definition and Characteristics

## Current Wikipedia Definition (Vertebrate)

"In vertebrates, a **neuroblast** or **primitive nerve cell** is a [postmitotic cell](/wiki/G0_phase "G0 phase") that does not divide further, and which will develop into a [neuron](/wiki/Neuron "Neuron") after a [migration](/wiki/Cell_migration "Cell migration") phase."

## Key Characteristics

**Post-mitotic status:** Yes. The article explicitly states neuroblasts are "a postmitotic cell that does not divide further." [Citation 1-2]

**Development origin:** Neuroblasts "differentiate from radial glial cells and are committed to becoming neurons." [Citation 4] They form through "asymmetric division of radial glial cells." [Citation 5]

**Migration:** Yes. The text confirms neuroblasts "will develop into a neuron after a migration phase" and describes how "The neuroblast detaches from the epithelium and migrates while the radial glial progenitor cell produced stays in the lumenal epithelium."

## Summary

Vertebrate neuroblasts represent a critical developmental stage: post-mitotic precursor cells derived from radial glial cells that migrate to their destinations before maturing into neurons.

AnnotationAssertion(Annotation(oboInOwl:hasDbXref "PMID:21181888") Annotation(oboInOwl:hasDbXref "http://orcid.org/0000-0002-0819-0473") obo:IAO_0000115 obo:CL_0009091 "A stem cell found in the interstitial compartment of the neonatal testis; it is capable of self-renewal as well as differentiation into steroidogenic cells (adult Leydig cells). Intermediate stages of development include progenitor Leydig cells and immature Leydig cells.")
AnnotationAssertion(Annotation(oboInOwl:hasDbXref "PMID:26735013") Annotation(oboInOwl:hasDbXref "PMID:28753612") Annotation(oboInOwl:hasDbXref "PMID:31784286") Annotation(oboInOwl:hasDbXref "PMID:35830791") Annotation(oboInOwl:hasDbXref "PMID:38092908") obo:IAO_0000115 obo:CL_0020025 "A retinal ganglion cell type that has a large soma and a wide, monostratified dendritic arbor that stratifies in the inner sublamina of the inner plexiform layer, with dendrites located just proximal to the inner ChAT band (Krieger et al., 2017). It shows a sustained increase in firing rate to ON stimuli (Krieger et al., 2017). It corresponds to the G24 functional type (Baden et al., 2016; Goetz et al., 2022), the C43 transcriptomic cluster (Tran et al., 2019), and is considered the mouse ortholog of the primate ON midget retinal ganglion cell (Hahn et al., 2023). It is molecularly defined by expression of Spp1, Smi32, Brn3b, Calbindin, and Tbr2 (Krieger et al., 2017; Tran et al., 2019).")
AnnotationAssertion(Annotation(oboInOwl:hasDbXref "PMID:18757316") Annotation(oboInOwl:hasDbXref "PMID:21364219") Annotation(oboInOwl:hasDbXref "PMID:34044844") Annotation(oboInOwl:hasDbXref "PMID:37834236") obo:IAO_0000115 obo:CL_4030034 "A multiciliated epithelial cell located in the respiratory tract epithelium, characterized by a columnar shape and motile cilia on its apical surface. This cell develops through a highly orchestrated process, transitioning from a basal progenitor via an intermediate deuterosomal cell stage that generates centrioles essential for ciliogenesis.")
AnnotationAssertion(Annotation(oboInOwl:hasDbXref "PMID:25564622") Annotation(oboInOwl:hasDbXref "PMID:34044844") Annotation(oboInOwl:hasDbXref "PMID:35387001") Annotation(oboInOwl:hasDbXref "PMID:37834236") obo:IAO_0000115 obo:CL_4033048 "A respiratory epithelial cell derived from a basal cell, with a topographic nuclear position between the basal and luminal cells of the airway epithelium. This non-basal, intermediate progenitor cell has limited proliferative capacity and can differentiate into multiciliated, secretory, or rare airway cells (ionocytes, tuft cells, neuroendocrine cells). It shares some ultrastructural features with basal cells but lacks the defined characteristics of fully differentiated cellular phenotypes.")
AnnotationAssertion(Annotation(oboInOwl:hasDbXref "PMID:40475517") obo:IAO_0000115 obo:CL_4052070 "A bipotent progenitor cell within the human fallopian tube epithelium, characterized by the concurrent expression of epithelial (e.g., EpCAM/CD326) and endothelial (e.g., PECAM1/CD31) markers at both the cell surface and transcript levels. This cell has the capacity to differentiate into ciliated and secretory epithelial cells, as well as potentially endothelial/stromal lineages. Positioned at the apex of lineage bifurcation, the cell exhibits stem-like and endothelial features, representing an intermediate developmental state between undifferentiated progenitors and lineage-committed epithelial cells.")

content.content:
{"body":"**CL term**
neural progenitor cell

CL core textual definition contradicts CxG definition regarding the self-renewal capacity of neural progenitor cells.

**CL def:** An undifferentiated cell that originates from a neural stem cell and has the capacity to generate multiple types of lineage-restricted progenitors **but not to self-renew.**

**CxG def:**
NPCs are characterized by **their ability to self-renew through mitotic cell division** in a process known as proliferation and possess the potential to differentiate into specific types of neural cells such as neurons, astrocytes, and oligodendrocytes. The type and number of cells that an NPC can produce depend on temporal and spatial signals in the nervous system microenvironment.

It seems that NPCs typically have limited self-renewal capacity compared to NSCs and eventually commit to differentiation. Therefore, the phrase "not to self-renew" is not accurate. 

**Suggested revision of textual definition**
An undifferentiated cell derived from a neural stem cell, with a (limited?) capacity to self-renew and the ability to generate multiple types of lineage-restricted progenitors, contributing to the formation of neurons, astrocytes, and oligodendrocytes.

PMID: [37508487](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/37508487/)


","comments":[],"state":"CLOSED","title":"[Text def]  neural progenitor cell"}

input.command:
cat > /tmp/strategy_comment.md << 'EOF'
## Analysis of issue #2312 — neuroblast and NPC classification

I've reviewed the current state of the ontology, the original issue, the linked discussions (#757, #617, #2807, #2922), and the cited NPC review (Martínez-Cerdeño & Noctor 2018, [PMC6291443](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6291443/)). Posting analysis only — no PR.

### Status of the action items in this issue

| Action | Status |
|---|---|
| ~~place NB(V) under NPC~~ | Correctly crossed out by @dosumis — vertebrate NB is post-mitotic, NPCs divide |
| Clarify NPC definition (cells that divide) | **Partially done** — PR #2881 (closed #2807) revised the def to include "limited capacity to self-renew … ability to generate multiple types of lineage-restricted progenitors". Doesn't say "divides" explicitly, but implies it. |
| Add GMC develops_from NB(invertebrate) | **Done** — `CL_0000469 SubClassOf RO_0002202 some CL_0000338` is in the file |
| Add NB develops_from RGC | **Not done** — `CL_0000031` has no `develops_from` axiom |
| Add text def for forebrain NB | **Not done** — `CL_1000042` has only label + EquivalentClasses, no `IAO:0000115` |
| Update NB(V) def to match Wikipedia/review | **Not done** — `CL_0000031` def is still "A cell that will develop into a neuron often after a migration phase." (sourced GOC:NV + Wikipedia) |

### Deeper structural problems beyond the action list

These are issues I think are worth discussing before doing the surface-level edits:

**1. NB(V) children are inconsistent with a strict NB definition.** If NB(V) is tightened to "post-mitotic, lineage-restricted to neuron, migrates" (the Wikipedia/Sadler definition), then the current children don't fit:

  - `CL_0002362` cerebellar granule cell precursor (GCP) — its *own* definition says it "can differentiate into astroglial cells **and** granule cells" and GCPs are well-known to be highly proliferative in the EGL. Both proliferative and not lineage-restricted to neuron → **not a neuroblast in the strict sense.** Better fit: NPC / glial-restricted-or-bipotent precursor.
  - `CL_0002676` neural crest derived neuroblast — NC-derived precursors are proliferative and multipotent (sympathetic neurons, Schwann cells, melanocytes…). Calling these "neuroblasts" is more of a historical convention (e.g., neuroblastoma).
  - `CL_1000042` forebrain neuroblast — best fit for the strict definition (e.g., adult SVZ → RMS → OB neuroblasts), but has no text def.

**2. NPC is structurally orphaned.** `CL_0011020` has only one asserted child, `CL_0000469` (ganglion mother cell, arthropod). And GMC's parent `CL_0000338` (NB sensu invert) is itself a `neural stem cell`, not an NPC. So the NPC branch is essentially empty in the way the broader literature uses NPC. By contrast, recent CL additions like `CL_4042021` (neuronal-restricted precursor, NRP) and `CL_4042022` (astrocyte-restricted precursor) are under NPC's parent `progenitor cell` but **not under NPC** — even though they would be canonical NPC subtypes by the modern literature definition.

**3. Lineage chain is broken.** Per Martínez-Cerdeño & Noctor 2018, the canonical vertebrate cortical lineage is **NSC → RGC → IPC → neuroblast/post-mitotic neuron**. In CL today:
  - `CL_0000047` NSC `develops_from` neurectodermal cell ✓
  - `CL_0000681` RGC has no `develops_from` to NSC, no `subclass_of` NSC, no `subclass_of` NPC. It sits under glial cell with `develops_from` to neurecto-epithelial cell. (@dosumis suggested in #757 it should go under NSC.)
  - **No "intermediate progenitor cell" term exists** in CL despite being the dominant proliferative pool in cortical development.
  - `CL_0000031` NB(V) has no `develops_from` to RGC or IPC.
  - GCI: vertebrate neuron `develops_from` NB(V) ✓ (added in #757)

**4. NB(invertebrate) classified as NSC.** `CL_0000338` (NB sensu Nematoda+Protostomia) is `subclass_of CL_0000047` (NSC). This is correct per @dosumis (#757) — in flies/worms, neuroblasts *are* the neural stem cells. But it makes the term name "neuroblast" mean two completely different things across the two clades:
  - Invertebrate NB = stem cell, divides asymmetrically, makes GMCs
  - Vertebrate NB = post-mitotic, committed to neuron, migrates
  This is confusing for downstream users (especially scRNA-seq annotators who may not realize the sensu-distinction). CellxGene uses both `CL_0000031` and `CL_0000338` actively.

### Three options

#### Option A — Minimal (just close the action list)
Do exactly what's in the un-struck-through bullets:
1. Tighten NB(V) def using a recent review (replace Wikipedia source).
2. Add `CL_0000031 develops_from CL_0000681` (RGC) — possibly a GCI to avoid stranding NC-derived neuroblasts.
3. Add text def to `CL_1000042` forebrain neuroblast.
4. Add "by mitotic division" wording to NPC def.

- **Pros**: Self-contained, low risk, satisfies the explicit asks.
- **Cons**: Leaves the deeper inconsistencies untouched. NB(V) children still don't fit the tightened def. NPC is still essentially empty. RGC still floats free of NSC/NPC.

#### Option B — Restructure (recommended)
Do (A) plus:
5. **Reclassify CL_0002362 (GCP)** out from under NB(V). Move under NPC (or the new NRP-style branch) — rationale: it's proliferative and bipotent.
6. **Reclassify CL_0002676 (NC-derived NB)** similarly (proliferative, multipotent). Could become "neural-crest-derived neural progenitor" with NB as a related synonym for legacy.
7. **Place RGC under NSC** (per @dosumis #757) **and/or under NPC**. Several papers describe RGCs as "the primary NPCs" — so `RGC subClassOf NPC` may actually be the cleanest model.
8. **Add `intermediate progenitor cell` (IPC / Tbr2+ basal progenitor)** as a new term under NPC, and as the develops_from parent of NB(V) in cortex. Cite PMC6291443 / PMID:30574073.
9. **Reclassify CL_4042021 (NRP) and CL_4042022 (ARP) under NPC** rather than directly under progenitor cell. They are the canonical "lineage-restricted progenitors" that the NPC definition explicitly mentions.
10. Consider restoring the `CL_0011020 subClassOf CL_0000034` (stem cell) link is **NOT** appropriate (the new def explicitly says "limited capacity to self-renew" → so NPC is correctly *not* a stem cell — current modeling fine).

- **Pros**: Coherent hierarchy, matches modern literature, makes NPC a useful branch with real children, useful for cellxgene annotation. Disambiguates GCP from "true" neuroblasts.
- **Cons**: Larger scope, ~5–8 axiom changes plus possibly 1 new term (IPC). Reclassifications may surface as unexpected changes for downstream users tracking these terms.

#### Option C — Deprecate the NB(V) concept
Per the review, modern usage barely uses "neuroblast" as a developmental term outside specific contexts (adult SVZ neurogenesis, neuroblastoma). The strict Wikipedia definition essentially describes "post-mitotic newborn neuron" or "migrating neuronal precursor" — which could be modeled with more specific terms.

- Mark `CL_0000031` deprecated; replace with: (a) `intermediate progenitor cell` for proliferative children currently misclassified, (b) `migratory neuronal-restricted precursor` for the strict-sense NB role (adult SVZ → OB).
- Similarly fold `CL_0000338` more cleanly into invertebrate NSC structure.

- **Pros**: Cleanest, follows the principle stated in this issue ("fewer overlapping grouping concepts").
- **Cons**: Highly disruptive — `CL_0000031` is in the cellxgene_subset and is heavily annotated. NB(V) is also widely used by SS ontologies as a hook (per @dosumis's first comment). Deprecation would need careful migration plan.

### Recommendation

**Option B**, executed in two PRs:

- **PR-1 (definitional)**: Items 1–4 from Option A. Low risk, addresses ticket. Use Martínez-Cerdeño & Noctor 2018 (PMID:30574073) as a primary source — already cited in NPC def, so consistent across the branch.
- **PR-2 (structural)**: Items 5–9. Reclassify GCP, NC-derived-NB, RGC, NRP, ARP. Add IPC if there's appetite. Discuss first because it touches cellxgene-annotated terms.

Open question I'd like resolved before any PR:
- For `CL_0000031` NB(V), should the post-mitotic-and-committed definition be **enforced** (Option B/C: move proliferative children out) or **relaxed** to keep current children in place (a looser "vertebrate neural precursor" sense)? The issue title and @dosumis's clarifying comment lean toward enforcement, but moving GCP affects downstream annotations.

Also worth noting that #757 created a GCI saying "vertebrate neurons develop_from neuroblast(V)". If we enforce the strict NB(V) def AND keep this GCI, we are committing CL to the strong claim that *every* vertebrate neuron passes through a post-mitotic neuroblast stage. This is approximately true for adult SVZ neurogenesis but not for all neurogenesis (e.g., direct neurogenesis from RGC is documented). May want to revisit that GCI.

---

content.text:
Posted analysis on #2312 (no PR, as requested): https://github.com/obophenotype/cell-ontology/issues/2312#issuecomment-4362118799

**Summary of what I found and posted:**

- **Status of original action items**: 1 done (GMC develops_from invertebrate NB), 1 partially done (NPC def revised in PR #2881), 3 still open (NB(V) develops_from RGC, forebrain NB definition, NB(V) definition update).
- **Deeper structural problems** the original list didn't address: NB(V)'s children don't fit a strict NB definition (esp. cerebellar granule cell precursor — proliferative + bipotent); NPC has only one arthropod-specific child; RGC doesn't connect to NSC or NPC; no `intermediate progenitor cell` term exists; recently-added NRP/ARP terms aren't classified under NPC.
- **Three options laid out**:
  - **A** Minimal — just close the action list.
  - **B** Restructure (recommended) — A plus reclassify GCP/NC-derived-NB/RGC/NRP/ARP and add an IPC term. Suggested two-PR rollout.
  - **C** Deprecate `CL_0000031` entirely and replace with finer-grained concepts.
- **Open question** for @cmungall before any PR: enforce the strict post-mitotic-committed-to-neuron definition of NB(V) (which forces moving GCP and NC-derived NB out) or relax it to keep current children. Also flagged that the #757 GCI ("every vertebrate neuron develops_from a NB(V)") may need revisiting under the strict definition.
